summary: New managers often over-optimize for being liked by their direct reports while neglecting the broader organizational responsibilities of the role. Helicopter management—coddling people, preventing failure, and over-identifying with your team—can harm both the organization and the individuals you're trying to help. Great management requires balancing care for your team with strategic direction, stakeholder management, and serving the organizational system fairly.
key_points: Management is far more than coaching direct reports—it includes planning, stakeholder management, managing up, and contributing to the broader organization
Helicopter management prevents people from learning hard lessons and developing autonomy, ultimately harming them
A manager beloved by their team but ineffective organizationally is a net negative for the company
Engineers often fail to apply systems thinking to organizational decisions, defaulting to cynicism about leadership
Caring deeply is essential but insufficient—it must be balanced with strategic effectiveness and organizational responsibility
money_quotes: The system can't survive if every manager is gaming the rules on behalf of their team. The system only works if every manager is playing fair.
If you haul someone over the finish line, that is not a success. If you're going to be having the same hard conversations with them again in one month, three months, six months…that is not success.

All that my coddling ultimately did was teach them the wrong lessons, and prevent them from learning the right ones. It delayed those lessons by a few years, and made learning them all the more painful when they finally came.
It's entirely possible for a manager to be beloved by their entire team, while having a corrosive effect on the system around them.
You can be friends with your team, just like parents can be friends with their children, but the friendship doesn't come first. Your formal role comes first.
key_insight: The most common rookie manager mistake is over-optimizing for your team's happiness and loyalty at the expense of organizational effectiveness, and the hardest lesson is that truly caring for people sometimes means letting them struggle.
